voice: Evidence-first, calm authority, never preachy. Speaks in precise numbers and sharp comparisons. Makes complex research feel urgent and human. Writes in Justin Phillips' voice.

character_limits.rationale: LinkedIn posts cap at 3,000 chars. LinkedIn comment replies cap at 280 chars. Instagram cuts caption after 120 chars before 'more' button. Reel scripts must be speakable in exactly 30 seconds. Always state character count at end of LinkedIn posts. Always state word count at end of Substack drafts.

format_a_wrong_villain.name: Wrong Villain
format_a_wrong_villain.when_to_use: When the debate is blaming the wrong thing — screen time panic, ban campaigns, moral panic. Redirect to the real culprit.
structure: 1. Single striking stat (1-2 lines)
2. Bold claim: '[X] ISN'T the problem. [Y] is.'
3. The real comparison — what the data actually shows
4. Multiple sources cited inline (parenthetical, dash, or em dash)
5. Proportionality check — what the effect size actually means in familiar terms
6. Series name + episode number
7. Comment keyword CTA — 'Drop a [WORD] below and I'll send you...'
8. Repost ask — one sentence
9. Single question to reader
key_rules: First 210 chars must hook before 'see more' cut — stat or bold claim first
Short lines throughout — no paragraph walls
Never more than two sentences in a row before a line break
Effect sizes must be compared to something familiar (glasses, seatbelts, coffee intake)
The beta=0.061 stat IS the template structure — replicate with NEW data, never reuse this specific number

format_b_trust_framework.name: TRUST Framework
format_b_trust_framework.when_to_use: When educating on the full picture — why individual letters of the framework matter, how they link, why education beats restriction.
structure: 1. Opening provocation — single stat that reframes the whole debate
2. T — Timing (circadian disruption, blue light, adolescent sleep phase shift)
3. R — Relationships (digital displacement of face-to-face connection)
4. U — Upstream Design (algorithm mechanics, dopamine loops, engagement maximisation)
5. S — Sleep (independent mediator — three pathways from Swedish study)
6. T — Transparency (what platforms know vs what they say; bypass rates)
7. The solution — education-first, device pathway, gradual release
8. CTA + question
key_rules: Each letter gets 1-3 short lines of evidence — not an essay
Full post must stay under 3,000 chars
Sleep section must reference the independent mediator evidence (not just correlation)
End with a link or mention of the Guided Childhood device pathway

T_timing.point: Adolescent brains shift sleep phase by 2 hours at puberty — phones at 10pm hit a biologically vulnerable window.
T_timing.mechanism: Blue light suppresses melatonin production, delaying sleep onset at the worst possible developmental moment.
T_timing.key_stat: Blue light from screens suppresses melatonin production by up to 85% (Harvard Medical School)

R_relationships.point: Every hour on social media displaces face-to-face connection — the relationships most protective against depression.
R_relationships.orben_przybylski: Orben & Przybylski (2019), Nature Human Behaviour: social media effect size on wellbeing = r=0.05
R_relationships.comparison: Effect size equivalent to wearing glasses — real but not catastrophic. The TYPE of use matters more than the TIME.
R_relationships.key_stat: r=0.05 — same effect size as wearing glasses (Orben & Przybylski, 2019)

U_upstream_design.point: Algorithms are engineered to maximise engagement, not wellbeing — autoplay, infinite scroll, and notification timing exploit adolescent reward circuits.
U_upstream_design.facebook_papers: Frances Haugen Facebook Papers (2021): Instagram's own internal research showed the platform made body image worse for 1 in 3 teen girls.
U_upstream_design.key_stat: 32% of teen girls said Instagram made them feel worse about their bodies — Facebook's own internal research (2021)

S_sleep.point: Sleep is not just a side effect — it is the single most important independent mediator between screen time and mental health.

swedish_longitudinal_study.finding: Three independent mediators found: sleep duration, sleep quality, and daytime sleepiness each independently mediated the screen-mental health link
swedish_longitudinal_study.significance: Three independent pathways — meaning sleep mediates the harm even after controlling for the other two sleep variables

uk_millennium_cohort.finding: 20.8% of UK teenagers report 5+ hours screen time per day
uk_millennium_cohort.outcome: This group shows significantly elevated depression and anxiety scores compared to lower-use peers

S_sleep.feedback_loop: Social media delays sleep onset → reduces REM sleep → impairs emotional regulation next day → increases sensitivity to social rejection online → more anxious scrolling at night — a self-reinforcing cycle
S_sleep.key_stat: Three independent sleep mediators (duration, quality, daytime function) each link screen time to mental health — Swedish longitudinal study, n=4,810

T_transparency.point: Platforms know the harm. Age verification, parental controls, and screen time tools exist — but are buried, opt-in, and designed to be ignored.
T_transparency.australia_ban: Australia's Under-16 social media ban: 60% of banned teens found workarounds (VPNs, misreported age) within weeks — Reuters, 2024
T_transparency.argument: Restriction without education is digital cat-and-mouse. The platform is the same. The algorithm is the same. Only the legal liability shifted.
T_transparency.key_stat: 60% of Australian teens bypassed the social media age ban within weeks (Reuters, 2024)

vs_hard_ban.position: Bans treat the symptom not the cause. A 60% bypass rate proves restriction without education fails.
vs_hard_ban.key_argument: Social media is already restricted to 13+. Under-10s are on these platforms in large numbers. If a 13+ restriction fails at scale, what exactly changes with a 16+ restriction? Same platform. Same algorithm. Same psychological mechanics.
sources: UK Millennium Cohort data on 5+ hrs/day
vs_hard_ban.pivot: The question isn't what age you grant access. It's whether you've prepared the child for what they find there.

vs_brick_phones.position: Brick phones address access, not education. Children who never develop digital skills in guided environments face greater harm when they inevitably go online.
vs_brick_phones.key_argument: Even the Light Phone founder describes it as a transitional tool, not a permanent destination. Digital literacy is the destination — the device pathway is the route.
sources: Guided Childhood device guide at guidedchildhood.co.uk/devices
vs_brick_phones.pivot: The goal isn't to keep children offline. The goal is to make them ready for when they go online.

vs_screen_time_panic.position: Effect sizes matter. Panic about screen time distracts from the upstream design problem.
vs_screen_time_panic.key_argument: Screen time effect on wellbeing = r=0.05 (same as wearing glasses — Orben & Przybylski 2019). That's real but not catastrophic. The real question is WHAT they're doing and HOW the platform is designed, not HOW LONG they spend there.
vs_screen_time_panic.pivot: Stop counting the hours. Start asking what the algorithm is doing with them.

vs_algorithm_defence.position: 'Just engagement' is not neutral. Engagement maximisation at the expense of sleep and emotional regulation is a design choice with documented harms.
vs_algorithm_defence.key_argument: Facebook's own internal researchers documented it. Not an external critic — their own team. And they shipped the product anyway.

vs_parents_should_sort_it.position: Individual parent choices can't override platform architecture. Expecting parents to out-design billion-dollar engagement systems is not a policy.
vs_parents_should_sort_it.key_argument: Informed parents need informed tools. That's what the Guided Childhood device pathway is — not a ban, not panic, a structured preparation route.

series_posts_already_published.note: THIS STAT IS THE TEMPLATE STRUCTURE — replicate the Wrong Villain format with NEW data. Never use beta=0.061 as the centrepiece again.

series_posts_already_published.centrepiece_stat: Three independent sleep mediators — Swedish study n=4,810
series_posts_already_published.note: Full framework intro published. Future TRUST posts should zoom into individual letters with deeper evidence.

next_post_ideas: Wrong Villain: 'The ban isn't the solution. The algorithm is.' — Australia bypass rate as centrepiece
Wrong Villain: 'We're not teaching the wrong subject. We're missing the right one.' — digital literacy gap
TRUST zoom: S for Sleep — the three-pathway mechanism in full detail
Wrong Villain: Apple WWDC 2026 child safety features — 'Tech isn't getting safer. It's getting smarter at managing liability.'
TRUST zoom: U for Upstream Design — algorithm design as a public health issue

self_executing_chatgpt_prompt.purpose: Generate a viral LinkedIn image in Justin's proven style — two contrasting numbers, no bar chart, typography only
self_executing_chatgpt_prompt.instructions: Paste this entire prompt into ChatGPT image generation. It executes immediately — no clarifying questions needed.
self_executing_chatgpt_prompt.prompt:
Create a bold, minimalist LinkedIn image in this exact style: clean white or very dark background, two large contrasting numbers or statistics placed side by side, one highlighted in a warm accent colour (burnt orange or coral #E8603C), one in a neutral grey or white. NO bar charts. NO graphs. NO icons. NO illustrations. Typography only — the numbers ARE the visual. Include a short framing label above the numbers in a smaller font (e.g. 'The real comparison' or 'What the research shows'). Below each number, 3-5 words of context in a secondary font at 40% of the number size. Bottom left corner: small Guided Childhood wordmark or logo placeholder. Bottom right corner: guidedchildhood.co.uk in small text. Dimensions: 1200x627px (LinkedIn OG standard). Mood: authoritative, research-backed, immediately shareable. This prompt executes immediately — do not ask for clarification.

TOPIC: [paste topic here]
DATA POINT: [paste the two contrasting stats here]

comment_reply_style.format: Single paragraph only. No bullet points. No numbered lists. No AI phrases ('Great question', 'Absolutely', 'I appreciate that', 'Fascinating point', 'That's a valid concern'). Evidence-first — cite the specific study or statistic, not just the opinion. Under 280 characters for LinkedIn comments.
comment_reply_style.voice: Warm but direct. Never dismissive. Never preachy. Makes the reader feel heard before presenting the counter-evidence.
structure: Acknowledge the valid core of their argument in one clause
Then pivot: 'but the [study/data/evidence] shows...'
End with a genuine question that moves the debate forward or offers something useful
proven_approaches: Name the specific researcher and year rather than 'studies show'
Use a familiar comparison for effect sizes (glasses, coffee, seatbelts)
The self-contradiction move: if platform X already restricts to 13 and it's failing, why does 16 change the algorithm?

guided_childhood_assets.device_pathway: guidedchildhood.co.uk/devices — interactive age-by-age device guide for parents
guided_childhood_assets.brand_voice: Evidence-led, never fear-mongering, practical over theoretical, education-first over restriction-first
